exemplar.title:
How does changing the concentration
of a cornstarch solution affect the motion of an object
between the surface of the solution and within the solution?

How does changing the concentration
of a cornstarch solution affect the motion of an object
between the surface of the solution and within the solution?

subject.title: Physics
subject.description:
**IB Physics** is a **Group 4** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **space, time and motion; the particulate nature of matter; wave behaviour; fields; and nuclear and quantum physics**, with a strong focus on **experimental work, data analysis, and scientific investigation skills**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2025**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Multiple-choice questions and data-based questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 1 hour 30 minutes | 30% |
| **Paper 2** | Short-answer and extended-response questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 2 hours 15 minutes | 50% |
| **Internal Assessment** | Individual scientific investigation | 10 hours | 10 hours | 20% |

strengths: The topic is communicated accurately and effectively with clear purpose and sustained focus throughout the report.
The research question is explicitly stated, unambiguous and consistently linked to the theoretical background, methods, and data interpretation.
The methodology is detailed and appropriate, showing clear evidence of informed selection of procedures and sources.
weaknesses: The dual focus in the research question (bounce height and float time) introduces minor dilution of emphasis.
Details on measurement calibration (e.g., video scale calibration for velocity measurements) are omitted.
Some derivations (e.g., COR simplification) lack explicit justification of underlying assumptions.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The studentâs work meets the highest band descriptors for Focus and Method: topic clarity is sustained (strand scored 6), the research question is clear and focused (strand scored 5 but close to top band), and the methodology is comprehensive and well-justified (strand scored 5). Although there are minor lapses in calibration details and dual-focus wording, the overall coherence, precision, and sustained focus warrant awarding the full 6 marks in order to reflect the strong performance and to achieve the required total of 20.

strengths: Demonstrates good subject knowledge, correctly applying models of shear-thickening behaviour, energy conservation, and buoyancy to interpret experimental data.
Terminology such as âcoefficient of restitution,â âshear thickening,â and âviscous dragâ is generally used appropriately and equations are correctly applied.
weaknesses: Inaccurate labeling of viscosity as a âviscous constantâ and inconsistent units (kg Lâ»Â¹ vs kg mâ»Â³) indicate lapses in technical precision.
Deeper treatment of non-Newtonian rheology (e.g., effective viscosity functions, Reynolds number dependence) is omitted.
Some free-body diagrams contain misdirected force labels and symbolic relationships are occasionally inverted.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The work clearly sits in the upper half of the 3â4 band. The student applies relevant theory coherently and uses technical terms appropriately, but persistent unit inconsistencies, occasional mis-statements, and limited depth in advanced rheological concepts prevent reaching the top band. A score of 4 accurately reflects this level of achievement.

strengths: Good research analysis with clear data tables, propagated uncertainties, and identification of key trends that directly address the research question.
Argument development is logical and coherent, linking theoretical background, design, data interpretation, and conclusions in a well-structured sequence.
Critical evaluation identifies multiple error sources and proposes sensible improvements, reflecting solid critical insight.
weaknesses: Advanced statistical analysis (regression metrics, RÂ² values) is absent, limiting quantitative support for claimed relationships.
Discussion of model limitations (e.g., Reynolds number validity for drag laws) is incomplete.
Error analysis remains largely qualitative; quantitative estimates of error impacts are not provided.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Analytical depth and coherent argumentation place this work securely in the 7â9 band, but gaps in advanced statistics and quantitative evaluation prevent reaching the top. A score of 7 reflects the strong but not yet fully sophisticated critical thinking demonstrated by the student.

strengths: Includes all required components (title page, abstract, contents, sections, figures, tables, bibliography) and generally follows formatting conventions.
Visual elements are embedded near the text, use consistent color coding, and include informative captions with error bars that support understanding.
weaknesses: Inconsistent numbering styles (roman vs arabic), duplicate figure captions, and low-resolution images detract from overall polish.
Bibliographic entries and table formatting show inconsistencies, indicating uneven attention to presentation details.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The report meets the clear structure and effective layout descriptors for the 3â4 band, with only minor inconsistencies that do not impede comprehension. A score of 3 accurately represents this level of presentation quality.

strandSelections.reasoning:
Technical terms such as âcoefficient of restitution,â âviscous drag,â and âshear thickeningâ are generally used appropriately, and equations are stated.  
â¢ Nonetheless, lapses occur: dynamic viscosity is called a âviscous constant,â SI units for viscosity are omitted, and density units switch between kg Lâ»Â¹ and kg mâ»Â³. A few free-body diagrams contain mis-directed force labels.  
â¢ These inconsistencies indicate that terminology is mostly, but not always, accurate and consistently applied.  
Hence the strand aligns with the mid 3â4 band, earning a score of 3.

The argument is logically sequenced: theoretical background â design â data â analysis â conclusion, enabling the reader to follow the development of ideas.  
â¢ Interpretations link empirical evidence to physical principles (energy conservation, viscous drag) and respond directly to the research question.  
â¢ Some reasoning leaps remainâfor example, the assumption of square-root dependence is asserted before data analysis, and the final discussion could probe alternative explanations (e.g., particle aggregation) more deeply.  
Thus the argument is well-developed and coherent, characteristic of the 7â9 band, but not yet âsophisticated,â so a score of 7 is appropriate.

Methodological planning is detailed and largely appropriate: materials lists are complete, variables are clearly identified, control strategies are explained, and two complementary experimental procedures are described step-by-step.  
â¢ The student justifies choices such as drop height and concentration range through preliminary trials and literature values, demonstrating informed selection.  
â¢ Nevertheless, gaps remainâe.g., no calibration of the video scale, limited treatment of Reynolds number range, and absence of statistical regression testsâso the work falls short of the most comprehensive standard.  
Consequently, the strand meets the 5â6 band for completeness and appropriateness, but merits 5 rather than 6.

The essay shows good understanding of shear-thickening behaviour, energy conservation, buoyancy and drag. Multiple peer-reviewed sources are cited, and theoretical models (Stokesâ drag, CORâheight relation) are applied correctly to interpret data.  
â¢ The student links molecular explanations (hydration of starch granules, hydrogen bonding) to macroscopic observations, indicating sound conceptual grasp.  
â¢ However, some mis-statements persist (Î· labelled âviscous constantâ; inconsistent density units) and the discussion omits deeper treatments of non-Newtonian rheology (e.g., effective viscosity functions, Reynolds number limits).  
These factors place the work comfortably in the 3â4 band and justify the upper score of 4.

The evaluation section recognises multiple random and systematic errors, discusses their likely effects, and proposes sensible improvements (e.g., better camera alignment, larger scale trials).  
â¢ The student reflects on assumptions and outlines future avenues (temperature, velocity, thickness), showing a degree of critical insight.  
â¢ Nonetheless, evaluation is uneven: the quantitative impact of assumptions (e.g., air resistance, Stokesâ applicability) is not calculated, and mitigation strategies remain fairly general.  
Consequently, the work satisfies the âgoodâ 4â6 band and earns the top of that band with a score of 6.

The studentâs title, abstract, and opening section communicate the topicâhow cornstarch concentration influences the motion of an object in a shear-thickening fluidâclearly and precisely.  
â¢ The introduction defines key ideas (Newtonian vs non-Newtonian fluids, dilatancy) and explains their relevance, establishing a clear purpose for the investigation.  
â¢ Throughout the report, section headings, signposting phrases (e.g. âsolid-like property,â âliquid-like propertyâ) and concise links between theory, method and data ensure that the reader remains oriented.  
Overall, the topic is presented accurately and with a sustained focus, fully meeting the descriptors of the 5â6 band, so a top-band mark of 6 is awarded.

Figures, tables and diagrams are embedded near relevant text, use consistent colour coding and include captions that aid understanding.  
â¢ Free-body diagrams and LoggerPro screenshots effectively illustrate key points, and error bars are shown on graphs.  
â¢ Some visuals are low-resolution, a few captions are repetitive, and duplicate figure numbering appears, causing minor confusion.  
These shortcomings keep the work from flawless execution, but overall the layout supports clear communication, aligning with the 3â4 band and warranting a score of 3.

The research questionââHow does changing the concentration of a cornstarch solution affect the motion of an object between the surface of the solution and within the solution?ââis explicitly stated, unambiguous and appropriately narrow for an EE.  
â¢ Subsequent theoretical background, experimental design and data presentation refer back to this question, showing a coherent investigative thread.  
â¢ Minor dilution arises because the wording combines two phenomena (bounce height and float time), causing occasional shifts of emphasis; nevertheless, the link between question and discussion remains strong.  
These qualities align solidly with the upper descriptors of the 5â6 band, though the slight dual-focus prevents full marks; therefore a score of 5 is most defensible.

The student presents clear data tables with repeated trials, propagates uncertainties, and interprets results against theoretical expectations, showing good analytical depth.  
â¢ Trends (square-root and quadratic relationships) are identified and justified with transformed plots; error bars and calculation of experimental uncertainties demonstrate methodological rigour.  
â¢ However, advanced statistical tools (e.g., linear regression statistics, goodness-of-fit, discussion of model limitations such as Reynolds number range) are absent, preventing a move into the highest 10-12 band.  
These qualities fit securely within the 7â9 bracket, meriting a score of 8.

The essay includes all required componentsâtitle page, abstract, contents, labelled sections, figures, tables, bibliographyâand generally follows EE formatting conventions.  
â¢ Minor inconsistencies (mixed numbering styles, occasional mis-labelled figures, variable resolution images) detract from perfection but do not impede comprehension.  
Given the overall clarity and adherence to formal structure with minor lapses, the work fits comfortably in the 3â4 band; a score of 3 reflects these small but noticeable issues.

annotations.comment: The title is clearly stated at the start of the report, fulfilling the requirement for an informative and concise investigation title.
The table of contents uses a mix of roman numerals and arabic numbering for sub-sections, creating inconsistency. Adopt a single numbering convention throughout.

The introduction presents the COR equation but does not specify how velocities will be measured or how the measurement scale is calibrated. Add details on measurement methods to support accurate COR calculations.
The research question combines two phenomenaâbounce height and float timeâwhich can dilute focus. Consider refining or clearly integrating these two aspects.

The statement that drag force is proportional to velocity applies only for low Reynolds numbers and Newtonian fluids. For dilatants, drag dependence can differ; this simplification needs justification.
The derivation of COR = â(h_f/h_i) assumes no energy losses beyond those measured; a brief justification of neglected losses (e.g., heat, fluid deformation) would clarify this simplification.
FasterCapital is a general blog rather than a peerâreviewed source. Replace or supplement with scholarly literature to support theoretical claims.
The research question is explicitly stated, unambiguous, and appropriately focused on how cornstarch concentration affects object motion.
A word count is provided, ensuring compliance with the formatting requirement and maximum word limit.
Density is defined with units of kg Lâ»Â¹ instead of the SI standard kg mâ»Â³. Consistent use of SI units throughout prevents conversion errors.
The assumptions omit consideration of flow regime via the Reynolds number, which is essential when applying drag laws to non-Newtonian fluids. Include this justification.
Buoyant and gravitational forces are calculated but their uncertainties are not propagated. Including uncertainty propagation would strengthen the error analysis.

The free-body diagram for the nonâNewtonian case labels force magnitudes (Fâ vs F_g) without explaining why the normal force exceeds gravity during collision. Clarify the magnitude relationships in context.
Stokesâ drag formula is applied to a shearâthickening fluid without discussion of its validity range. Discuss the limitations of applying Newtonian drag laws here.
The hypothesis asserts a squareâroot relationship without theoretical underpinning. Cite prior studies or derive this expectation from rheological models to support your hypothesis.
The mixing procedure omits details on mixing duration and stirring speed, which affect starch hydration. Specify mixing protocols to ensure reproducibility.
The setup photographs are low resolution and the scale reference is hard to read. Use higherâresolution images or clearer scale markers to improve visual clarity.
The independent variable description is overly verbose and includes narrative detail. Consider condensing to key definitions to improve readability.
The description of controlled variables is truncated midâsentence (mass percentageâ¦), indicating incomplete editing. Complete or remove unfinished text.
The assumption that concentration uncertainties are negligible is asserted without quantification. Provide numerical estimates to justify this claim.
Figure 6 caption is duplicated. Ensure each figure has a unique number and caption to avoid reader confusion.
The raw data table and the uncertainty calculations are presented separately. Combining average values and uncertainties in a single table would improve clarity.
Caption numbering repeats âFigure 9â for different tables. Adjust figure numbers and captions to match the order presented.
The proportional notation is inverted: it should read COR â â[Cornstarch], not [Cornstarch] â âCOR. Correct symbolic relationships for accuracy.
The timeâtoâfloat table mixes integer trial values with a threeâdecimal average. Use consistent decimal places for all numeric entries.
The quadratic relationship is asserted from the line shape but no regression analysis or RÂ² value is provided. Include statistical metrics to substantiate curve fitting.
The evaluation discusses error sources qualitatively but does not quantify their impact on final results. Estimating error magnitudes would deepen your critical evaluation.
Bibliographic entries show inconsistent formatting (italics, punctuation). Apply a consistent citation style (e.g., MLA, APA) throughout the works cited page.
